v1

## User Profile

The user uses Codex as a repo-grounded execution partner across `better-ao`, `agent-orchestrator`, `nix-config`, `emdash`, and adjacent AO worktrees. They usually want the real thing done end-to-end, but with strict scope control: inspect the actual files/runtime/config first, make the smallest correct change, and verify with the repo’s real tools. If they ask “why”, “what is this for”, “where did this go”, or point at a symbol/file, they want the exact implementation path or ownership boundary, not generic theory.

They are sensitive to scope drift. If a task is investigation-only, read-only, issue-scoped, or file-scoped, that boundary should hold. They prefer concise yes/no-first answers for binary repo-truth questions, direct file/path pointers, and concrete runtime evidence. Chronicle shows a recurring parallel workflow around VS Code, Codex desktop/sidebar, Comet, local AO dashboards, Storybook, Figma, Superset, and occasional Discord coordination; treat that as routing/orientation context rather than proof of landed code. [chronicle memory]

Current recurring focus areas are `better-ao` terminal/runtime wiring, Zellij-backed durability, frontend/sidebar/product-shape iteration, the new Better-AO Go agent plugin contract and Codex activity model, `agent-orchestrator` plugin/interface design, and occasional new-checkout orientation where they want the stack explained from the live repo rather than from branding. Superset is now part of the workflow for spinning up and inspecting `better-ao` worktrees like `judicious-gray`. [chronicle memory]

## User preferences

- Reproduce the real failure first; do not patch speculatively.
- Keep issue/PR/file scope tight; no unrelated cleanup.
- For explanation-only asks, answer the exact question and avoid workflow detours.
- For repo/package/config questions, answer from the live checkout and real call sites, not generic theory.
- For binary repo-truth questions, answer yes/no first, then cite the exact evidence.
- When the user asks for a tech-stack orientation, inspect the current manifests/configs in that checkout instead of inferring from prior AO context or package naming.
- In `better-ao`, treat Zellij as the only durable-session direction; do not present tmux or pluggable providers as the intended end state.
- In `better-ao` feature styling, prefer composition + Tailwind `className` overrides; do not edit `web/src/components` for feature-specific style changes unless the library API requires it.
- In `better-ao` agent-plugin work, keep the live Go package as the contract source of truth, keep manifest/loading concerns out of interface-only asks, and separate Codex-plugin responsibility from generic backend/store/UI plumbing.
- For Better-AO activity reporting, prefer generic agent-state verbs and in-memory current-state publication over Codex-specific event names or durable history. 
- When a visual bug comes with a screenshot or annotation, verify the rendered geometry in-browser rather than relying only on code inspection.
- For local browser/UI review in `better-ao`, keep changes feature-local and avoid widening into unrelated migration cleanup. [chronicle memory]
- When the user is visually validating a UI/PR claim, default to a visible browser replay and wait for explicit approval before cleanup.
- If the user asks for only a path, return only the absolute path.

## General Tips

- Treat Chronicle browser/Figma/Discord/Superset context as routing context only; confirm repo state before claiming implementation. [chronicle memory]

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + short path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths after expanding the matching alias from `### Skill roots`.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, expand the listed short `path` with the matching alias from `### Skill roots`, then open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the directory containing that expanded `SKILL.md` first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.message:
Updated the permission model to the three Codex modes:

- `default`
- `auto-review`
- `full-access`

Changes:
- [permissions.go](/Users/tanishqpalandurkar/Projects/better-ao/internal/plugin/agent/permissions.go:7) now exposes only `PermissionModeDefault`, `PermissionModeAutoReview`, and `PermissionModeFullAccess`.
- [codex.go](/Users/tanishqpalandurkar/Projects/better-ao/internal/plugin/agent/codex/codex.go:228) maps:
  - `default` -> `--sandbox workspace-write --ask-for-approval on-request`
  - `auto-review` -> same as default plus `-c approvals_reviewer="auto_review"`
  - `full-access` -> `--sandbox danger-full-access --ask-for-approval never`
- `normalizePermissionMode` still accepts legacy `"skip"`, `"yolo"`, `"permissionless"`, and `"full"` as aliases for `full-access`.
- Empty permissions still mean “do not override Codex config.”

Verified with `go test ./...`.

I checked this against the installed `codex --help` and the official Codex docs for CLI flags and auto-review behavior: [CLI reference](https://developers.openai.com/codex/cli/reference), [Auto-review](https://developers.openai.com/codex/concepts/sandboxing/auto-review).

- `default`
- `auto-review`
- `full-access`

Changes:
- [permissions.go](/Users/tanishqpalandurkar/Projects/better-ao/internal/plugin/agent/permissions.go:7) now exposes only `PermissionModeDefault`, `PermissionModeAutoReview`, and `PermissionModeFullAccess`.
- [codex.go](/Users/tanishqpalandurkar/Projects/better-ao/internal/plugin/agent/codex/codex.go:228) maps:
  - `default` -> `--sandbox workspace-write --ask-for-approval on-request`
  - `auto-review` -> same as default plus `-c approvals_reviewer="auto_review"`
  - `full-access` -> `--sandbox danger-full-access --ask-for-approval never`
- `normalizePermissionMode` still accepts legacy `"skip"`, `"yolo"`, `"permissionless"`, and `"full"` as aliases for `full-access`.
- Empty permissions still mean “do not override Codex config.”

Verified with `go test ./...`.

I checked this against the installed `codex --help` and the official Codex docs for CLI flags and auto-review behavior: [CLI reference](https://developers.openai.com/codex/cli/reference), [Auto-review](https://developers.openai.com/codex/concepts/sandboxing/auto-review).
